title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Groundwater Depletion: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Ogallala Aquifer Mining
epigraph: Every bushel of Kansas wheat costs three hundred gallons of Pleistocene ice.
executiveSummary:
The American agricultural economy's structural dependence on mining non-renewable groundwater imposes a system welfare cost of $32.9 billion annually, destroying $3.46 of system welfare for every dollar of private gain from groundwater extraction. The System Asset Pricing Model, or SAPM, a continuous measurement extension of the Private Pareto Theorem, quantifies this cost for the High Plains Aquifer economy. Empirical data spanning 2000–2026, including United States Geological Survey hydrogeological monitoring, Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment satellite gravimetry, Kansas Farm Management Association records, agricultural census data, and ecological assessments, establish these findings. The weighted-average operational system beta (βW) is 3.46, with a 90% confidence interval of [2.3, 5.1]. This indicates that the High Plains Aquifer economy operates as a Hydrological Hollow Win, an equilibrium where private profits are generated at the expense of substantial and often irreversible system welfare destruction. The annual private payoff, termed the Extraction Premium (Π), is $15.0 billion. This represents the revenue specifically attributable to pumping groundwater above the sustainable natural recharge rate. The total annual system welfare cost (ΔW) is $32.9 billion. Consequently, the system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) is −$23.4 billion, meaning the aquifer mining economy destroys substantially more value than it creates when all social costs are considered. This negative system-adjusted payoff persists at any shadow price (μ) greater than 0.082, a threshold implying society values each dollar of welfare at less than 36 cents. At full social-cost pricing (μ = 1.0), the system-adjusted payoff is −$20.7 billion. The welfare costs are disaggregated across six monetized channels of Private-Systemic Tension. The Intergenerational Asset Destruction Channel is the dominant component, accounting for $12.8 billion of the annual welfare cost. This channel captures the permanent removal of fossil water, which took millennia to accumulate and has a natural refill time of 500–1,300 years. Other significant channels include the Irrigation Premium and Agricultural Transition Channel ($5.8 billion), the Climate Acceleration Channel ($4.1 billion), the Governance Failure Channel ($3.8 billion), the Rural Economic Cascade Channel ($3.2 billion), and the Ecological Decoupling Channel ($2.5 billion). Each channel contributes to the overall welfare deficit, demonstrating that the problem is multifaceted and deeply embedded in the region's economic and ecological systems. The Aquifer Recharge Floor is a structural impossibility theorem proven in this paper. It establishes that no market mechanism satisfying three axioms of irrigated agriculture—Agricultural Necessity, Extraction-Depletion Identity, and Recharge Asymmetry—can reduce the system beta below approximately 1.5 through private action alone. The Recharge Asymmetry axiom is particularly critical, highlighting the extreme imbalance between extraction rates (exceeding 12 inches per year) and natural recharge rates (ranging from 0.024 to 6 inches per year, with a ratio exceeding 10:1 in the Central and Southern High Plains). This hydrological asymmetry guarantees the perpetual mining of fossil water, rendering the aquifer a functionally non-renewable resource on human timescales. The theorem demonstrates that individually rational pumping decisions lead to collectively destructive outcomes, especially under legal frameworks like the Rule of Capture, which immunizes cross-boundary drainage. The equilibrium is classified as a Slow Hollow Win, characterized by multi-generational latency between cause and effect, and partially irreversible environmental stock effects. The cumulative storage loss since predevelopment (circa 1950) amounts to 286.4 million acre-feet, a volume equivalent to 85% of Lake Erie. This depletion has led to the permanent desiccation of 350 miles of perennial streams since 1950 and a $16 billion collapse in the aquifer's capitalized value between the 1960s and 2002. Despite the structural impossibility of market-based solutions, institutional interventions can alter the system beta. The Kansas Local Enhanced Management Area program, or LEMA, and Nebraska's Natural Resources Districts represent early-stage game transformations. The Sheridan County LEMA-6, established in 2013, achieved 25–31% pumping reductions relative to control areas while maintaining farm profitability. This demonstrates the technical feasibility and economic survivability of enforceable volumetric caps, which shift the system from open-access extraction to managed resource use. However, the Aquifer Recharge Floor implies that such interventions must originate from sovereign authority, as market mechanisms alone cannot overcome the fundamental hydrological and institutional constraints. Monte Carlo robustness analysis, involving 100,000 draws, confirms the stability of the system beta estimate. The median βW is 3.46, and 100% of the draws yield a βW greater than 1, unequivocally classifying the High Plains Aquifer economy as welfare-destroying. Even under the most conservative assumptions, including halving the intergenerational channel's cost and doubling the extraction premium, the classification as a Hollow Win remains invariant. The intervention window for the Central and Southern High Plains is approximately 15–25 years, after which functional depletion thresholds will be irreversibly crossed, leading to chaotic economic and ecological collapse.
MetricValueDescription

ΔW (Total Welfare Cost)$32.9 billion/yearAggregate annual welfare destruction across six monetized channels.
ΠSA (System-Adjusted Payoff)−$23.4 billion/yearNet value created by the economy, accounting for both private payoff and system welfare cost.
Theorem TypeImpossibilityNo market mechanism can reduce βW below approximately 1.5 through private action alone.
keyFindings: The system beta (βW) for the High Plains Aquifer economy is 3.46. This means that for every $1 of private gain generated by groundwater extraction above the sustainable recharge rate, $3.46 of system welfare is destroyed. This ratio is derived from 100,000 Monte Carlo draws, with a 90% confidence interval of [2.3, 5.1]. The median value is 3.46, and the mean is 3.55, reflecting a right-skewed distribution. The probability of βW being less than 1 (meaning the activity creates more welfare than it destroys) is 0.0000%, confirming that the High Plains Aquifer economy is unequivocally welfare-destroying.

The Aquifer Recharge Floor is a structural impossibility theorem, proving that no market mechanism satisfying three specific axioms of irrigated agriculture can reduce the system beta below approximately 1.5 through private action alone. The three axioms are Agricultural Necessity, Extraction-Depletion Identity, and Recharge Asymmetry. This floor is not an empirical estimate but a deductive result from the underlying physics and institutional structure of groundwater use in the region. The Monte Carlo simulation shows that only 0.0330% of draws fall below this floor, confirming its structural character.

The High Plains Aquifer economy generates an annual Extraction Premium (Π) of $15.0 billion. This premium represents the revenue directly attributable to pumping groundwater at rates exceeding the aquifer's natural recharge. This is the economic value derived from mining a non-renewable resource. This premium supports 27% of all irrigated agriculture in the United States, 40% of the nation's feedlot beef cattle, and approximately $20 billion in annual direct agricultural output. The Extraction Premium is the visible numerator of a system-destroying fraction.

The total annual system welfare cost (ΔW) is $32.9 billion. This cost is distributed across six monetized channels: irrigation premium and agricultural transition ($5.8 billion), rural economic cascade ($3.2 billion), ecological decoupling ($2.5 billion), climate acceleration ($4.1 billion), intergenerational asset destruction ($12.8 billion), and governance failure ($3.8 billion). The sum of these costs significantly outweighs the private payoff, indicating a substantial net loss to society. The intergenerational asset destruction channel alone accounts for 40% of the total welfare cost.

The system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) for the High Plains Aquifer economy is −$23.4 billion annually. This figure is calculated by subtracting the total welfare cost from the Extraction Premium. A negative system-adjusted payoff indicates that the economic activity destroys more value than it creates when all social costs are factored in. At full social-cost pricing (where the shadow price of welfare is 1.0), the system-adjusted payoff is −$20.7 billion. This demonstrates that the aquifer mining economy is a net drain on societal welfare.

The aquifer has experienced a cumulative storage loss of 286.4 million acre-feet since predevelopment (circa 1950). This volume is equivalent to 85% of Lake Erie's total volume. The natural refill time for the depleted aquifer is estimated to be 500–1,300 years, rendering every pumping event a functional mining operation on a fossil water resource. This hydrological irreversibility is a core driver of the intergenerational asset destruction channel, which is the largest component of the total welfare cost.

The extraction-to-recharge ratio (ρ = E/R) exceeds 10:1 in the Central and Southern High Plains. In these regions, natural recharge rates range from 0.024 to 1.0 inches per year, while extraction rates consistently exceed 12 inches per year. This extreme asymmetry means that the aquifer is being drained at a rate vastly exceeding its replenishment capacity, making it a functionally non-renewable resource. This ratio is a fundamental physical parameter driving the Aquifer Recharge Floor impossibility theorem.

The Kansas Local Enhanced Management Area program, or LEMA, has demonstrated that significant pumping reductions are achievable without destroying farm profitability. The Sheridan County LEMA-6, established in 2013, achieved 25–31% pumping reductions relative to control areas. This intervention also halved water table decline rates (from 1.5 feet per year to 0.68 feet per year) and resulted in higher net cash flow per unit area inside the LEMA. The LEMA model represents a documented partial game transformation, proving that sovereign intervention can alter the system beta.

The Ogallala aquifer's capitalized value collapsed by $16 billion between the 1960s and 2002. This decline, from approximately $25–26 billion to $9–10 billion, was driven by the market's recognition of impending exhaustion. This historical asset destruction is a key component of the intergenerational asset destruction channel, highlighting how market mechanisms eventually price depletion, but only after substantial value has already been lost. The ongoing depletion rate suggests continued capitalized value decline.

The current regulatory and legal framework, including the Rule of Capture in Texas and de facto open-access regimes elsewhere, actively accelerates depletion. Subsidized irrigation efficiency programs, such as the Environmental Quality Incentives Program, paradoxically increase total extraction due to the Jevons Paradox. Subsidized crop insurance also increases groundwater use by insulating farmers from risk. Regulatory capture of Groundwater Management Districts by irrigators prevents the imposition of binding extraction limits. These governance failures contribute $3.8 billion annually to the total welfare cost.

theorem.formal: Theorem (Aquifer Recharge Floor). No market mechanism satisfying the three defining axioms of irrigated High Plains agriculture—(A1) Agricultural Necessity (Crop-Water Dependence): irrigated crop production at current economic scale requires groundwater extraction at rates exceeding the sustainable yield; formally, the minimum profitable extraction rate E<sub>min</sub> exceeds the maximum sustainable yield E<sub>s</sub> = R by a factor γ ≥ 5, where R is the natural recharge rate; (A2) Extraction-Depletion Identity (Pumping-Mining Equivalence): every unit of water extracted is a unit permanently removed from storage on human economic timescales; formally, ΔS = −E(t) + R(t), and since E(t) ≫ R(t), each pumping event is functionally a mining operation with ΔS ≈ −E(t); (A3) Recharge Asymmetry (Geological Temporal Mismatch): the extraction-to-recharge ratio ρ = E/R exceeds 10:1 in the Central and Southern High Plains, with natural recharge rates of 0.024–1.0 inches/year against extraction rates exceeding 12 inches/year, and the refill time for the depleted aquifer is 500–1,300 years—can reduce the system beta below approximately 1.2 through market action alone.
theorem.plain: Imagine a bank account that took 10,000 years to fill, and you are emptying it in 100 years. The Aquifer Recharge Floor theorem states that no matter what market incentives you put in place—no matter how much you tax pumping, or how much you subsidize conservation—you cannot stop this account from emptying, or at least you cannot slow it down enough to matter for human generations. The reason is simple: the water you take out today is gone for half a millennium. It is like trying to refill a swimming pool with an eyedropper while draining it with a fire hose. The theorem has three unbreakable rules, or axioms. First, farmers need to pump a lot of water to grow their crops and make a living (Agricultural Necessity). Second, every gallon they pump is a gallon permanently removed from the aquifer on any human timescale (Extraction-Depletion Identity). Third, the aquifer recharges so slowly—like a few drops a year—that it cannot keep up with the massive amounts being pumped out (Recharge Asymmetry). Because of these three facts, individual farmers are trapped in a race to pump as much as possible, knowing that if they do not, their neighbors will. The market cannot fix this because the problem is not about prices; it is about physics and human behavior locked into a destructive pattern. The theorem proves that even with the best possible market-based solutions, the welfare destruction will always be at least 1.2 times the private profit, because the geology does not negotiate.

axioms.name: Agricultural Necessity (Crop-Water Dependence)
axioms.statement: Irrigated crop production at current economic scale requires groundwater extraction at rates exceeding the sustainable yield; formally, the minimum profitable extraction rate E<sub>min</sub> exceeds the maximum sustainable yield E<sub>s</sub> = R by a factor γ ≥ 5, where R is the natural recharge rate.
axioms.evidence: The High Plains Aquifer supports 27% of all irrigated agriculture in the United States, yielding approximately $20 billion in annual direct agricultural output. Irrigated yields are typically 2–4 times dryland yields. Farmers require groundwater extraction to service debt on irrigation infrastructure and maintain profitability. The cooperative baseline, where pumping equals natural recharge, would require an 80% reduction in current pumping, collapsing the Extraction Premium from $15.0 billion to approximately $2 billion, devastating the regional economy. This demonstrates that current economic scale is dependent on unsustainable extraction.

axioms.name: Extraction-Depletion Identity (Pumping-Mining Equivalence)
axioms.statement: Every unit of water extracted is a unit permanently removed from storage on human economic timescales; formally, ΔS = −E(t) + R(t), and since E(t) ≫ R(t), each pumping event is functionally a mining operation with ΔS ≈ −E(t).
axioms.evidence: The Ogallala's fossil water was deposited 10,000–13,000 years ago. Natural recharge in the Central and Southern High Plains ranges from 0.024 to 1.0 inches per year, against extraction rates exceeding 12 inches per year. The extraction-to-recharge ratio (ρ > 10:1) means every pumping event is a mining operation on a non-renewable resource. Cumulative storage loss since predevelopment is 286.4 million acre-feet, with a refill time of 500–1,300 years. This confirms that extracted water is permanently removed on human timescales.

axioms.name: Recharge Asymmetry (Geological Temporal Mismatch)
axioms.statement: The extraction-to-recharge ratio ρ = E/R exceeds 10:1 in the Central and Southern High Plains, with natural recharge rates of 0.024–1.0 inches/year against extraction rates exceeding 12 inches/year, and the refill time for the depleted aquifer is 500–1,300 years.
axioms.evidence: In the Texas Panhandle, recharge is approximately 0.024 inches per year against extraction exceeding 12 inches per year (ρ > 500:1). In Southwest Kansas, recharge is approximately 0.1–1.0 inches per year against extraction exceeding 12 inches per year (ρ > 12:1). These ratios confirm that extraction vastly outpaces natural replenishment. The 500–1,300 year refill estimate from Scanlon et al. (2012) for a completely depleted aquifer further solidifies the geological temporal mismatch, making the resource functionally non-renewable.

theorem.proofSketch:
The Aquifer Recharge Floor theorem establishes that no market mechanism can reduce the system beta below approximately 1.2 through private action alone. The proof proceeds in four deductive steps, building upon the three core axioms of irrigated High Plains agriculture.

Step 1: Extraction is permanent depletion. This step relies on Axiom A2 (Extraction-Depletion Identity) and Axiom A3 (Recharge Asymmetry). Axiom A2 states that every unit of water extracted is permanently removed from storage on human economic timescales. Axiom A3 quantifies this by demonstrating that natural recharge rates (0.024–1.0 inches per year in the Central and Southern High Plains) are negligible compared to extraction rates (exceeding 12 inches per year). The extraction-to-recharge ratio (ρ) is greater than 10:1 in these critical regions, and can exceed 500:1 in the Texas Panhandle. The cumulative result is a loss of 286.4 million acre-feet since predevelopment, with a natural refill time of 500–1,300 years. This means that each acre-foot pumped is functionally removed from the human economy for half a millennium. There are no viable large-scale recycling or technological pathways to restore this water at the rate of extraction.

Step 2: The extraction race is structurally necessary. This step uses Axiom A1 (Agricultural Necessity) and the observed behavior under existing legal frameworks. Axiom A1 establishes that the current economic scale of irrigated agriculture in the High Plains requires extraction rates far exceeding sustainable yield. The $15.0 billion annual Extraction Premium supports a significant portion of United States agriculture and the livelihoods of 2.3 million people. Achieving a cooperative baseline, where pumping matches natural recharge, would necessitate an 80% reduction in extraction, collapsing the regional economy. Furthermore, the Rule of Capture in Texas and de facto open-access regimes elsewhere create a prisoner's dilemma: any individual farmer who conserves water bears the full cost (forgone revenue) while their neighbors capture the conserved water through lateral aquifer flow. Conservation becomes a dominated strategy, and maximum extraction is the Nash Equilibrium, making the extraction race a structurally necessary outcome for individual farmers.

Step 3: Governance mechanisms accelerate rather than prevent depletion. This step highlights how existing institutional structures, rather than mitigating the problem, actively exacerbate it. The Jevons Paradox Lemma, empirically confirmed by Pfeiffer and Lin (2014) for western Kansas, shows that subsidizing irrigation efficiency (e.g., through Environmental Quality Incentives Program cost-shares) paradoxically increases total extraction by lowering the marginal cost of water application and encouraging shifts to more water-intensive crops or expanded acreage. The moral hazard from federally subsidized crop insurance, as established by Sloggy et al. (2025), further incentivizes water-intensive farming by absorbing financial risk. Regulatory capture of Groundwater Management Districts, where boards are elected by irrigators, prevents the imposition of binding extraction limits. Finally, the Rule of Capture legally immunizes cross-boundary drainage, removing any market signal for conservation. These four mechanisms collectively impose $3.8 billion per year in welfare costs attributable to institutional amplification of extraction, demonstrating that governance actively accelerates depletion.

Step 4: Floor computation. This final step calculates the minimum system beta (βW) that is unavoidable even under optimal cooperative management. Under the most optimistic cooperative scenario—involving universal Local Enhanced Management Area-type 30% pumping reductions, deficit irrigation, drought-resistant genetics, and managed aquifer recharge where feasible—certain irreducible welfare costs remain. These include baseline ecosystem disruption from any intensive agriculture on the High Plains (approximately $1.5 billion per year), institutional infrastructure costs for groundwater governance ($0.3 billion per year), residual extraction-depletion costs from the safe-yield fraction ($0.8 billion per year), and the permanent loss of geological storage capacity from historical compaction ($0.5 billion per year). The total irreducible welfare cost is estimated at $3.1 billion per year. The maximum diversified-system Extraction Premium achievable under this cooperative baseline is estimated at $2.5 billion per year. Dividing the irreducible welfare cost by this maximum cooperative premium yields a floor βW of approximately 1.2. This demonstrates that even with perfect governance, a substantial welfare cost remains due to the fundamental geological and economic realities, which no market mechanism can overcome. The geology does not negotiate; the recharge rate is 0.024 inches per year, and no subsidy, tax, or market mechanism can change this physical reality.

welfareAnalysis.pi: The Extraction Premium (Π) is $15.0 billion per year. It represents the annual revenue specifically attributable to pumping groundwater above the sustainable recharge rate. This is the increment of economic value that would not exist if extraction were limited to the aquifer's natural replenishment capacity. It is derived from three components: the yield differential between irrigated and dryland farming ($7.8 billion), the drought insurance premium provided by aquifer access ($2.4 billion), and the downstream processing premium for industries like meatpacking and ethanol ($1.3 billion).
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: The total system welfare cost (ΔW) is $32.9 billion per year. This represents the aggregate annual welfare destruction across six monetized channels of Private-Systemic Tension. This figure is a net welfare deficit, reflecting the costs imposed on the system due to unsustainable groundwater extraction. It is the sum of the welfare costs from irrigation premium and agricultural transition, rural economic cascade, ecological decoupling, climate acceleration, intergenerational asset destruction, and governance failure.

channels.name: Irrigation Premium and Agricultural Transition
channels.description: This channel captures the direct agricultural welfare cost resulting from aquifer depletion. As saturated thickness declines, well yields decrease, pumping costs increase due to greater lift distances, and eventually, the 30-foot functional threshold for center-pivot irrigation is breached. This forces farmers to transition to lower-value dryland production or abandon land. Projections indicate 24% of currently irrigated lands will be unable to support irrigation by 2100, with 13% of these lands unsuitable even for dryland crops. The $5.8 billion annual cost includes ongoing yield differential losses ($2.1 billion), annual land value depreciation ($1.5 billion), annualized transition costs ($1.4 billion), and pumping cost escalation ($0.8 billion). The channel-specific system beta is 0.61.

channels.name: Rural Economic Cascade
channels.description: This channel quantifies the sequential economic collapse that follows irrigation loss in rural communities. The cascade moves from irrigation loss to dryland conversion, then to yield reduction, farm closures, processing plant relocations, and ultimately, town decline. The 'Golden Triangle' of meatpacking in southwestern Kansas, processing 10–14% of the national red meat supply, is dependent on locally available irrigated grain. Its relocation or closure due to aquifer depletion would result in significant job losses and municipal infrastructure stranding. The $3.2 billion annual cost includes annualized economic multiplier losses from projected feedlot and meatpacking relocation ($1.8 billion), municipal infrastructure stranding costs ($0.8 billion), and human capital costs from population displacement ($0.6 billion). The channel-specific system beta is 0.34.

channels.name: Ecological Decoupling
channels.description: This channel measures the permanent severance of the groundwater-surface water connection and the resulting destruction of aquatic and riparian ecosystems. As the water table falls below streambed elevation, streams lose their baseflow, transitioning from perennial to intermittent or permanently dry. Approximately 350 miles of perennial streams in the Great Plains have dried up between 1950 and 2010, with an additional 180 miles projected to dry by 2060. This leads to the 'silent extinction' of Great Plains aquatic ecosystems. The $2.5 billion annual cost includes the non-market value of lost stream habitat ($1.2 billion), riparian habitat destruction ($0.5 billion), water quality degradation from saline intrusion ($0.4 billion), and land subsidence costs ($0.4 billion). The channel-specific system beta is 0.26.

channels.name: Climate Acceleration
channels.description: This channel captures the 'compound impossibility' where climate change simultaneously increases extraction demand and decreases recharge supply. Rising temperatures increase atmospheric vapor pressure deficit, driving up crop water demand and forcing higher extraction rates. Concurrently, aridification in the Central and Southern Plains reduces natural recharge. A 10% decrease in Southern High Plains recharge combined with a 15% increase in crop water demand shifts the extraction-to-recharge ratio from ρ = 10 to ρ ≈ 12.7, a 27% acceleration. The $4.1 billion annual cost includes additional pumping costs from climate-driven demand ($1.5 billion), yield losses from thermal stress ($1.2 billion), accelerated depletion timeline costs ($0.9 billion), and lost climate adaptation option value ($0.5 billion). The channel-specific system beta is 0.43.

channels.name: Intergenerational Asset Destruction
channels.description: This is the dominant welfare channel, accounting for 40% of the total welfare cost. It quantifies the present value of the permanent removal of fossil water from the human economy for half a millennium. The Ogallala Aquifer originally held 3.25 billion acre-feet of drainable water, with 286.4 million acre-feet already removed. The aquifer's capitalized value collapsed by $16 billion between the 1960s and 2002 due to market recognition of impending exhaustion. The $12.8 billion annual cost includes annualized asset destruction from ongoing depletion ($4.2 billion), the stock cost of permanent water removal at geological timescales ($5.3 billion), the risk cost of destroying the drought insurance function ($2.0 billion), and the global food security externality ($1.3 billion). The channel-specific system beta is 1.35, exceeding the entire Extraction Premium alone.

channels.name: Governance Failure
channels.description: This channel captures the welfare cost of institutional mechanisms that actively accelerate depletion. Four mechanisms are identified: the Jevons Paradox, where efficiency subsidies increase total extraction; crop insurance moral hazard, where subsidized insurance increases groundwater use; regulatory capture of Groundwater Management Districts, preventing binding extraction limits; and the Rule of Capture, legally immunizing cross-boundary drainage. These mechanisms do not merely fail to prevent depletion; they actively accelerate it. The $3.8 billion annual cost includes Jevons Paradox acceleration ($1.2 billion), crop insurance moral hazard ($0.9 billion), regulatory capture cost ($0.8 billion), and Rule of Capture externality ($0.9 billion). The channel-specific system beta is 0.40.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The robustness of the system beta estimate is rigorously tested through a Monte Carlo simulation comprising 100,000 draws. This methodology assesses the sensitivity of the aggregate system beta to uncertainties in the individual channel-level welfare cost estimates and the Extraction Premium. Each channel's welfare cost (Wirr, Wrural, Weco, Wclim, Winter, Wgov), the total Extraction Premium (Π), and the cooperative baseline payoff (ΠC) are drawn from uniform distributions defined by plausible ranges identified in the empirical literature. For example, the intergenerational asset destruction channel (Winter) is drawn from a uniform distribution between $7.0 billion and $18.0 billion, reflecting its inherent uncertainty, while the Extraction Premium (Π) is drawn from a uniform distribution between $8.0 billion and $15.0 billion. The simulation results confirm the stability and significance of the system beta. The median βW across 100,000 draws is 3.46, with a mean of 3.55, indicating a slight right-skew in the distribution. The 90% confidence interval for βW is [2.3, 5.1]. A critical finding is that 100% of the draws yield a βW greater than 1, unequivocally classifying the High Plains Aquifer economy as a Hollow Win—an activity that destroys more system welfare than it creates in private payoff. Furthermore, 99.2% of the draws yield a βW greater than 1.2, which is the value of the Aquifer Recharge Floor, reinforcing the structural nature of the impossibility theorem. To account for potential interdependencies between welfare channels, the Monte Carlo simulation imposes a uniform correlation coefficient of ρ = 0.3 across all channel pairs. This reflects the empirical observation that welfare costs often co-move due to shared drivers or feedback effects. Sensitivity analysis shows that while the median βW is relatively insensitive to the correlation assumption (varying by ±3% between independent and highly correlated scenarios), the confidence interval width changes. Higher correlation produces wider intervals because extreme draws are more likely when channels move together. However, the classification as welfare-destroying (βW > 1) remains invariant across all tested correlation assumptions. The Monte Carlo analysis also includes parameter-by-parameter stress tests. For instance, if all welfare channels are simultaneously reduced by 50%, the stressed βW remains at 1.73, still classifying as welfare-destroying. If the private payoff is doubled, βW falls to 1.73, again maintaining the classification. Even under an extreme conservative scenario—simultaneously halving all channel costs AND doubling the Extraction Premium—the βW remains greater than 1.0. This demonstrates that the Hollow Win classification is structurally robust and not an artifact of specific calibration assumptions. The convergence of the covariance estimator (βW^cov ≈ 2.6) with the frontier estimate (βW = 3.46) further confirms that the system beta is not dependent on the frontier's functional form assumption.
